section.title: The Reign of Uzziah
section.sectionRationale: As long as Uzziah sought God, he prospered remarkably, defeating enemies, fortifying Jerusalem, developing agriculture, and building one of Judah's strongest armies. But his very success became his undoing: he grew proud, entered the temple to burn incense reserved for priests alone, and was struck with leprosy on the spot, spending the rest of his life isolated from the house of the LORD he had once diligently sought.
section.thesis: Uzziah's reign shows that the same success God grants in response to seeking Him can, left unchecked, curdle into the pride that assumes it may cross any boundary, so that the king most helped by God becomes the king most decisively humbled by Him.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Uzziah's diligent seeking of God and remarkable prosperity, verses 1 to 15; his pride and unlawful incense offering, verses 16 to 20; and his lifelong leprosy and isolated death, verses 21 to 23.

units.label: Uzziah reigns in Judah
units.note: as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success

verses.text: All the people of Judah took Uzziah, who was sixteen years old, and made him king in place of his father Amaziah.
Uzziah was the one who rebuilt Eloth and restored it to Judah after King Amaziah rested with his fathers.
Uzziah was sixteen years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem fifty-two years. His mother's name was Jecoliah; she was from Jerusalem.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Amaziah had done.
He {{darash|sought}} God throughout the days of Zechariah, who instructed him in the fear of God. And as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success.

units.label: Uzziah's achievements
units.note: his fame spread far and wide

verses.text: Uzziah went out to wage war against the Philistines, and he tore down the walls of Gath, Jabneh, and Ashdod. Then he built cities near Ashdod and among the Philistines.
God helped him against the Philistines, against the Arabs living in Gur-baal, and against the Meunites.
The Ammonites brought tribute to Uzziah, and his fame spread as far as the border of Egypt, for he had become exceedingly powerful.
Uzziah built towers in Jerusalem at the Corner Gate, the Valley Gate, and the angle in the wall, and he fortified them.

Since he had much livestock in the foothills and in the plain, he built towers in the desert and dug many cisterns. And since he was a lover of the soil, he had farmers and vinedressers in the hill country and in the fertile fields.
Uzziah had an army ready for battle that went out to war by assigned divisions, as recorded by Jeiel the scribe and Maaseiah the officer under the direction of Hananiah, one of the royal officers.
The total number of family leaders of the mighty men of valor was 2,600.
Under their authority was an army of 307,500 trained for war, a powerful force to support the king against his enemies.
Uzziah supplied the entire army with shields, spears, helmets, armor, bows, and slingstones.

And in Jerusalem he made skillfully designed devices to shoot arrows and catapult large stones from the towers and corners. So his fame spread far and wide, for he was helped tremendously until he became powerful.

units.label: Uzziah's pride and unlawful offering
units.note: he was unfaithful to the LORD

verses.text: But when Uzziah became powerful, his arrogance led to his own destruction. He was {{maal|unfaithful}} to the LORD his God, for he entered the temple of the LORD to burn incense on the altar of incense.
Then Azariah the priest, along with eighty brave priests of the LORD, went in after him.

They took their stand against King Uzziah and said, "Uzziah, you have no right to offer incense to the LORD. Only the priests, the descendants of Aaron, are consecrated to burn incense. Leave the sanctuary, for you have acted unfaithfully; you will not receive honor from the LORD God."

Uzziah, with a censer in his hand to offer incense, was enraged. But while he raged against the priests in their presence in the house of the LORD before the altar of incense, {{tsaraat|leprosy}} broke out on his forehead.
When Azariah the chief priest and all the priests turned to him and saw his leprous forehead, they rushed him out. Indeed, he himself hurried to get out, because the LORD had afflicted him.

units.label: Uzziah's isolation and death
units.note: cut off from the house of the LORD

verses.text: So King Uzziah was a leper until the day of his death. He lived in isolation, leprous and cut off from the house of the LORD, while his son Jotham had charge of the royal palace and governed the people of the land.
As for the rest of the acts of Uzziah, from beginning to end, they are recorded by the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz.
And Uzziah rested with his fathers and was buried near them in a field of burial that belonged to the kings, for the people said, "He was a leper." And his son Jotham reigned in his place.

darash.gloss: To seek, inquire, or resort to. Uzziah darash God throughout the days of his mentor Zechariah, and the text draws a direct line, as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success; the whole chapter turns on what happens when that seeking stops.

maal.gloss: Unfaithfulness, treachery, or sacrilege. Uzziah's entering the temple to burn incense reserved for priests is called maal, the same term used elsewhere for sacrilegious trespass against what is holy, not a minor procedural error but a serious violation of God's own order for worship.

tsaraat.gloss: Leprosy, or a severe skin disease as defined in Leviticus 13. Tsaraat breaks out on Uzziah's forehead at the very moment of his rage against the priests, an immediate and visible divine judgment that permanently separates him from the temple he had just violated.

basic.context: Uzziah becomes king at sixteen and reigns fifty-two years, seeking God under the mentorship of Zechariah and enjoying remarkable success: military victories over the Philistines, Arabs, and Ammonites, extensive building projects, agricultural development, and one of the strongest armies Judah ever fielded. But success breeds pride, and Uzziah enters the temple to personally burn incense, a role reserved exclusively for Aaron's priestly descendants. When the priests confront him, he rages against them, and leprosy breaks out on his forehead immediately, in the temple itself. He is rushed out, lives the rest of his life isolated and cut off from the house of the LORD, and his son Jotham governs in his place until his death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative illustrating a clear and sobering principle: the very blessings God grants in response to faithful seeking can, if unchecked by humility, become the occasion for pride that oversteps God's clearly defined boundaries. The chapter is not a claim that all illness is direct punishment, but it does show that God takes seriously the distinct roles He assigns, even to kings whom He has greatly blessed.
basic.originalLanguages: Uzziah's whole prosperous era is summarized by darash, seek, as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success. His downfall is named maal, unfaithfulness or sacrilege, a serious trespass against what belongs to God alone. The judgment is instant and visible: tsaraat, leprosy, breaking out precisely where his pride had led him, in the temple itself.
basic.typology: Uzziah's exclusion from the temple due to leprosy, a condition that rendered a person ceremonially unclean and cut off from the assembly, anticipates the deeper truth that sin itself defiles and separates from God's presence, while Christ's own willingness to touch lepers and make them clean, Mark 1:41, reverses this pattern entirely, bringing the excluded near rather than driving them away.
basic.christ: Uzziah, blessed as long as he sought God, fell the moment pride led him to claim a role, the priestly offering of incense, that was never his to claim. This contrasts sharply with Christ, our true High Priest, who alone has the right to enter God's presence and intercede, and who freely shares that access with His people not by their usurping a forbidden role but by His own atoning work, which tears the temple veil and welcomes us in, Hebrews 4:14-16.
basic.god: God rewards those who genuinely seek Him with real, tangible blessing, showing that seeking the LORD is never wasted. Yet God is also the one who fiercely guards the boundaries of true worship; even a greatly blessed and long reigning king cannot claim what belongs to the priesthood alone without swift and visible consequence, showing that closeness to God's blessing is never the same as license to redefine His holy order.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 16 tells of Korah's rebellion, another instance of someone outside the priesthood claiming its exclusive privileges and facing severe judgment. Leviticus 13 gives the regulations concerning leprosy that explain Uzziah's total exclusion from worship. Proverbs 16:18 states that pride goes before destruction, exactly Uzziah's pattern. Hebrews 4:14-16 presents Christ as the true and final High Priest through whom all believers now have confident access to God.
basic.oneStory: Uzziah's long, God blessed reign, undone in a single act of pride that claimed a priestly role never granted him, warns every reader that success and humility do not automatically travel together. His story anticipates the need for a true King who is also a true and rightful Priest, Jesus Christ, in whom the boundary Uzziah violated is not erased but fulfilled and opened to all who come to God through Him alone.

context.literary: The chapter's structure itself embodies its lesson: verses of steady, expanding success, verse 5's summary as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success, and then an abrupt pivot at verse 16, but when Uzziah became powerful, his arrogance led to his own destruction, followed by swift and permanent consequence.
context.historical: Uzziah's military and building achievements, including advanced siege devices to shoot arrows and catapult stones, reflect a genuinely significant reign, corroborated by his mention in Isaiah's inaugural vision, Isaiah 6:1, which is dated to the year King Uzziah died, marking him as a major figure of the eighth century.
context.canonical: Uzziah's leprosy and lifelong exclusion from the temple stands alongside Korah's rebellion, Numbers 16, and Saul's unlawful sacrifice, 1 Samuel 13, as one of Scripture's clearest warnings against claiming for oneself a role God has reserved for others, regardless of one's other achievements or blessings.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Uzziah's genuine and extensive prosperity, granted as long as he sought the LORD, was ultimately undone by the pride that led him to claim the priestly role of burning incense, resulting in swift, visible, and permanent judgment that separated him from the very temple he had presumed to enter.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical judgment on a specific unlawful act; it is not suggesting every case of illness reflects direct personal sin. What endures is the sober principle that God's blessings do not grant license to redefine the roles and boundaries He has established, and that pride, even in a long faithful life, remains a real and present danger.

debates.question: Why would a king who had sought God so diligently for decades suddenly commit such a serious act of sacrilege?

views.label: Success gradually eroding genuine humility over time
views.case: Verse 16 explicitly ties the offense to the moment Uzziah became powerful, suggesting a slow accumulation of pride across years of unbroken success rather than a single sudden lapse, illustrating how prosperity itself, unchecked, can corrode character gradually.

views.label: A specific temptation toward greater religious authority
views.case: Some understand Uzziah's act as reflecting a broader ancient near eastern pattern where kings also functioned in priestly or cultic roles, suggesting Uzziah may have felt entitled, given his unmatched achievements, to claim a religious authority some neighboring rulers exercised, unaware or unconcerned that Israel's covenant explicitly separated kingship from priesthood.

debates.bearing: Either way, the account warns that no measure of past faithfulness or achievement grants permission to cross a boundary God has clearly established, and that pride can overtake even a long tested and genuinely devoted heart.

study.originalLanguages: Uzziah's entire era of blessing is summarized by darash, seek, as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success, verse 5. His downfall is explicitly named maal, sacrilegious unfaithfulness, when he claims the priestly incense offering for himself. The judgment, tsaraat, leprosy, appears immediately and visibly, permanently separating the once blessed king from the temple he had entered in pride.

typology.type: The king struck with leprosy and cut off from the temple because of his own pride
typology.fulfillment: Christ's cleansing touch that reaches lepers and the excluded, drawing near rather than driving away, Mark 1:40-42.

typology.type: The strict boundary between kingship and priesthood that Uzziah violated
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the perfect union of King and Priest in one person, Hebrews 7:1-3, fulfilling both roles without contradiction or usurpation.

study.christ: Uzziah's tragedy is that he sought a priestly privilege, offering incense in God's presence, that was never his by right, and was struck with a disease that cut him off from that very presence for the rest of his life. Christ stands in complete contrast and fulfillment: as the great High Priest who has passed through the heavens, He has the rightful and eternal access Uzziah wrongly claimed, and He shares that access freely with all who come to Him, so that we may draw near with confidence to the throne of grace, Hebrews 4:14-16, rather than being driven from it.
study.god: God rewards genuine, sustained seeking with real and visible blessing, as Uzziah's long prosperous reign shows. Yet God guards the distinct roles and boundaries He has established with equal seriousness, and He will not allow even the most accomplished and long faithful king to redraw those lines without swift, visible, and permanent consequence.

crossReferences.note: Korah's rebellion, another account of someone outside the priesthood claiming its exclusive privileges and facing severe divine judgment.
The regulations concerning leprosy explaining the total, lifelong exclusion from worship and community Uzziah suffers.
Isaiah's inaugural vision, dated to the year King Uzziah died, situating this chapter within the broader prophetic and historical record.
Christ, our great High Priest, through whom believers now have confident access to God's presence, the access Uzziah wrongly claimed for himself.

study.oneStory: Uzziah's reign, blessed as long as he sought the LORD and shattered the moment pride led him to claim a priestly role never granted him, warns that success without humility is a dangerous combination even for the most devoted. His leprous exclusion from the temple anticipates the far greater welcome Christ extends, the true King and true Priest in one, who draws near to the excluded rather than being driven from them, and grants access to God that no pride need, and no sin should, ever presume to seize for itself.

sermon.title: The Weakness of Power
sermon.why: Begg expounds 2 Chronicles 26:1-23, tracing how Uzziah's God given strength and success curdled into the pride that led to his downfall, matching this movement's passage and its central warning exactly.

quiz.q: What did the LORD grant Uzziah as long as he sought Him?

options.text: Success, including military victories, extensive building projects, and a powerful, well equipped army.
options.note: Verses 5 to 15 detail this extensive success explicitly tied to his seeking God.

options.text: Immediate and permanent immunity from any future failure.
options.note: The success was real but conditioned on continued seeking, as the chapter's second half tragically demonstrates.

options.text: Only personal wealth with no military or agricultural success.
options.note: The success described includes military victories, building, and agriculture, far broader than personal wealth alone.

options.text: The text does not describe any resulting blessing.
options.note: Verses 5 to 15 give a detailed account of Uzziah's success.

quiz.q: What specific act of pride led to Uzziah's downfall?

options.text: He entered the temple of the LORD to personally burn incense, a role reserved exclusively for the priests.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly names this unlawful act as his unfaithfulness.

options.text: He refused to pay tribute to neighboring nations.
options.note: The text specifies an act of religious sacrilege, entering the temple to burn incense, not a matter of tribute or foreign relations.

options.text: He built too many towers and fortifications.
options.note: His building projects are described positively as part of his success, not as the cause of his downfall.

options.text: No specific act of pride is described.
options.note: Verse 16 explicitly names his unlawful incense offering as the specific act.

quiz.q: How did the priests respond when Uzziah entered the temple to burn incense?

options.text: Azariah the priest and eighty brave priests confronted him, telling him he had no right to offer incense and must leave the sanctuary.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record this direct, courageous confrontation.

options.text: They allowed him to proceed without objection.
options.note: The priests explicitly confronted and opposed him rather than allowing the unlawful act to proceed unchallenged.

options.text: They immediately killed him for the offense.
options.note: The priests confronted him verbally and sought to remove him; his judgment came directly from God through leprosy, not execution by the priests.

options.text: No priests were present in the temple at the time.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly describes eighty priests present and confronting the king.

quiz.q: What happened to Uzziah at the very moment he raged against the priests?

options.text: Leprosy broke out on his forehead immediately, while he was still standing before the altar of incense.
options.note: Verse 19 records this immediate, visible divine judgment.

options.text: He calmly repented and left the temple peacefully.
options.note: The text describes him raging in anger and being struck with leprosy, not a calm, peaceful repentance.

options.text: He was struck blind rather than afflicted with leprosy.
options.note: The specific affliction named in the text is leprosy on his forehead, not blindness.

options.text: Nothing happened to him at that moment.
options.note: Verse 19 explicitly records the immediate outbreak of leprosy.

quiz.q: How did Uzziah spend the rest of his life after this judgment?

options.text: He lived in isolation, leprous and cut off from the house of the LORD, while his son Jotham governed the people.
options.note: Verse 21 records this lifelong isolation and his son's assumption of governing duties.

options.text: He continued reigning normally from the palace with full public duties.
options.note: The text explicitly describes isolation and exclusion from the temple, with Jotham taking over governance, not a continuation of normal royal duties.

options.text: He was executed shortly after being diagnosed with leprosy.
options.note: He lived the rest of his natural life in isolation rather than being executed for the offense.

options.text: No further details are given about his remaining years.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly describes his isolated remaining years and his son's governance.

questions: Uzziah prospered as long as he sought the LORD, but success itself became the occasion for pride. What blessing in your own life might be quietly tempting you toward self reliance rather than continued dependence on God?

The priests risked the king's anger to confront his sacrilege directly. Is there a wrong you have been avoiding confronting, in yourself or someone else, because it feels too costly or risky to speak up?

Uzziah's judgment was immediate and visible, cutting him off from the very place he loved. Where might God be calling you to respect a boundary He has set, even if you feel qualified or entitled to cross it?

Despite his tragic end, Uzziah's earlier decades of seeking God produced real, lasting good for his kingdom. How does this encourage you that seeking God is never wasted, even if your story includes later failure?

Christ is both the true King and the true Priest, holding together what Uzziah wrongly tried to combine himself. How does resting in Christ's finished access to God, rather than trying to secure your own, change the way you approach Him today?

translationNotes.note: Uzziah is also called Azariah elsewhere, most notably in 2 Kings 14:21 and 2 Kings 15:1-7, a common feature of variant royal names in the biblical record.
The foothills renders the Hebrew Shephelah or lowlands, referring to the western foothills of Judea where Uzziah's agricultural developments were concentrated.

Leprosy translates tsaraat, a term covering various skin diseases as defined in Leviticus 13, not necessarily identical to modern clinical leprosy, but carrying the same severe consequence of ritual impurity and exclusion.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 26:1-23. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter.
